title: The Knees and the Instinct to Collapse or Run
researcherTopic: sensorimotor psychotherapy, the body in trauma treatment
metaDescription: The body's memory of trauma is held in the knees, creating an instinct to either run or collapse. Learn how to heal this internal conflict.
imageDescription: Golden light illuminates the intricate grain of a wooden floor, where a pair of bare feet are firmly and gently planted. The scene evokes a feeling of profound grounding, safety, and the quiet strength of returning home to the body. The light is warm and forgiving, casting soft shadows.
htmlContent:
The body doesn't keep score. It is the score. Every sensation, every tension, every involuntary brace is a word in a language far older than thought, a direct communication from the deepest parts of our organismic intelligence.

The Body’s Ancient Language of Threat

We are a culture entranced by narrative, conditioned to believe that memory is a function of the mind, a vast and searchable library of filed-away stories and images that we can, with some effort, retrieve and inspect. But this is a strikingly incomplete picture. The oldest stories, the ones that shape our perception of the world from the inside out, are not stored in the language of words but in the language of pure sensation, in the deep, pre-verbal archives of the body. This isn’t a poetic metaphor; it is the sober reality of our physiological inheritance. It is a memory held not in the cerebral cortex but in the cerebellum, the brainstem, and the very tissues of the body. Long before the thinking brain evolved its intricate capacity for storytelling, the nervous system was already mastering a far more fundamental grammar... the grammar of survival. And much of that language is written in the muscles, bones, and connective tissues of the lower body, the structures designed to carry us toward safety or hold us in the suspended animation of a threat too large to fight or flee.

The hips, the powerful flexors of the legs, and most deeply, the knees, serve as the engine of our most primal survival responses. The knees, elegant in their biomechanical complexity, are designed for both rapid mobilization and sudden, complete immobilization. They are the hinge upon which the decision to run, to stand firm, or to collapse is physically enacted. In my years working in this territory, I've seen how the body tells a story the person cannot yet put into words. The story is often one of a significant and unresolved conflict between two of the nervous system’s most powerful and ancient directives: the instinct to run for one’s life and the instinct to collapse in on oneself to survive an overwhelming force. It turns out to be not a story of past events, but a living, breathing reality unfolding in the present moment, in the felt sense of the body itself.

When 'Run' Becomes an Internal Marathon

For many who have navigated the space of complex trauma, the flight response is not a singular event in the past but a chronic, ongoing condition of the present. The original threat may be decades gone, but the command to “run” continues to echo through the nervous system, a silent and exhausting internal marathon. This emerges not as a conscious desire to sprint from the room, but as a pervasive and deeply unsettling restlessness, an inability to truly settle into the moment, into one’s own skin. It's the fidgeting foot, the drumming fingers, the constant need to be busy, to be productive, to be moving, lest the stillness allow something far more uncomfortable to surface. That becomes the signature of a flight response that was initiated but never completed, never brought to a resolution of safety.

The Gravity of Collapse

To frame the buckling of the knees as a moment of weakness is to fundamentally misunderstand the intelligence of the body. It is a highly sophisticated, energy-conserving response designed to maximize the chances of survival in the face of overwhelming and inescapable threat. It is the body wisely pulling its own plug. For those with a history of complex trauma, this dorsal state can become a familiar, almost gravitational pull, a retreat from a world that feels too demanding, too activating, too unsafe. Learning about understanding the nervous system's hierarchy of response is the first step in recognizing that this collapse is not a character flaw, not a moral failing, but a physiological state with a deep and intelligent history. It is the body’s wisdom, even if it feels like a prison.

Think about that for a second.

The Knees as a Crossroads of Intention

The knees, in the context of a traumatized nervous system, become a physical and metaphorical crossroads, the precise anatomical location where the body’s most contradictory intentions collide. They are the battlefield where the sympathetic nervous system’s desperate scream to “run!” meets the dorsal vagal system’s significant, gravity-like pull to “collapse!” One part of the organism is flooring the accelerator while another is simultaneously slamming on the brakes. It is the energy of a panic attack, trapped inside a body that is simultaneously shutting down.

The felt sense of this internal conflict is one of trembling, of a deep and unnerving weakness in the legs, a sense of being utterly unable to stand and yet incapable of truly resting. The knees may literally shake, caught between the opposing commands to mobilize and immobilize. What we find here is the state that so many describe as feeling “stuck,” and it is not a psychological metaphor but a physiological reality. This state is often misdiagnosed as generalized anxiety, or a mood disorder, because its origins in the body's survival system are not understood. This is the territory where the practice of somatic experiencing becomes so vital, as it offers a way to begin to listen to these opposing signals not as a problem to be solved, but as a conversation to be witnessed. It is about bringing a gentle, non-judgmental curiosity to the trembling, to the weakness, to the striking sense of being caught between worlds, and allowing the body to tell its story in its own time, at its own pace.

Reclaiming the Right to Stand, and the Right to Rest

The path of integration and healing from this physiological schism is not about choosing one response over the other, not about valorizing the impulse to run or condemning the impulse to collapse. It is not about trying to eliminate these powerful, life-saving responses from our repertoire. Instead, the work is about slowly and patiently building the capacity within the nervous system to experience these energetic tides without being completely swept away by them. The path forward lies in the art of titration and pendulation, of touching into these difficult states for brief, manageable moments and then returning to a sense of relative safety and resource in the present. It is a gentle oscillation between the charge of the past and the ground of the present.

What emerges is the work of reclaiming two fundamental human rights that are so often stolen by trauma: the right to stand and the right to rest. The right to stand means feeling a sense of solidity in one’s own legs, a feeling of being grounded and present in the world, capable of meeting its challenges with a sense of agency. The right to rest means being able to yield to stillness and softness without that surrender being a trauma-induced collapse. This reclamation happens through micro-movements, through the gentle, exploratory work of feeling one’s feet on the floor, of noticing the support of the ground coming up to meet you, of allowing a small, intentional tremble without it cascading into a full-body tremor. It is a slow, deliberate process of re-inhabiting the lower body, of making it a safe and trustworthy place to reside. It is, in essence, a guide to somatic grounding, a path back to the earth beneath our feet, one sensation at a time.

What the Research Cannot Capture

Ultimately, this deep work with the body’s survival responses is not about fixing something that is broken. The nervous system is not a faulty machine in need of repair. It is a significantly intelligent and adaptive system that has done exactly what it needed to do to ensure our survival. The work, then, is one of relationship-building. It is a process of learning to listen to the stories the body is telling, not with an agenda of changing them, but with the simple, open-hearted intention of understanding them. It turns out to be how we begin to build a nervous system that has more choices, a system that is not locked into the binary of “run” or “collapse,” but has access to a whole spectrum of layered and creative responses to life. It's like learning the language of a country you were born in but never taught how to speak.

The question is not whether you are ready. It is whether you are willing. Those are different things, and the difference matters more than you think.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why do my legs feel so weak and shaky when I'm anxious?

That sensation of weakness and shakiness in the legs during moments of high anxiety or fear is the direct physical outcome of a nervous system in conflict. Your sympathetic nervous system is activating the powerful flight response, sending a surge of energy to your legs to prepare you to run. This collision of 'go' and 'stop' signals in your nervous system results in the trembling, weakness, or buckling sensation in your knees and legs. It's not a sign of actual physical weakness, but rather a sign of a system in a state of striking internal conflict.

Stay with me here.

Can I ever stop feeling this way?

The goal of this work is less about completely eliminating these feelings and more about building your capacity to experience them without being overwhelmed. These are, after all, essential survival instincts that are hardwired into our biology for a very good reason. Over time, through consistent and gentle practice, the nervous system learns it has more options than just full-throttle acceleration or a complete shutdown, allowing for a greater sense of ease, resilience, and choice in your daily life. The aim is not to silence the body's signals, but to become a more skilled and compassionate listener.

The body was never against you. It was only ever for you, using the only tools it had in its arsenal to keep you alive. The return home is simply learning to listen to what it was saying all along, beneath the noise of the story you thought was your life.

For those interested in exploring the physiological imprint of trauma, the resources provided by the National Institute of Mental Health offer a full and scientifically grounded overview of the current understanding of how overwhelming experiences shape our biology.

Here is the uncomfortable truth: understanding changes nothing without practice.
